moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn, staking our claim to the center and immediately controlling the d5 and f5 squares. This classical move frees our light-squared bishop and queen for potential development while creating space for our kingside pieces. The move sets up tactical themes around the center and prepares us for rapid piece development. Against most defenses, this leads to open, tactical positions where we can press for the initiative from move one.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1.e4 claims the center, controls d5 and f5, and opens lines for the queen and light-squared bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes at e4's support by attacking d4's control, forcing you to decide whether to defend the center or allow the tension to shape the position.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: c5 enters the Sicilian Snyder Variation — a known theoretical line.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Snyder Variation, a rare and somewhat provocative system that immediately fianchettoes our light-squared bishop to control the long diagonal. This move prepares Bb2, where our bishop will eye Black's kingside and put pressure on key central squares like e5 and f6. While this looks slow compared to typical Sicilian systems like the Open Sicilian (Nf3, d4), it can transpose into English Opening structures and keeps the position less theoretical. The downside is that we're not directly challenging Black's central control, giving our opponent time to establish their preferred setup, but we're building solid piece coordination and avoiding the main theoretical battles of the Sicilian.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: The Snyder Variation fianchettoes the light-squared bishop to Bb2, pressuring e5 and f6 while avoiding mainline Sicilian theory.
